---
title: "Testing without an exact oracle"
subtitle: "Testing training — Module 06"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    code-block-height: 400px
---

## Testing in bioinformatics: the same tools, an extra challenge

You know how to write tests for scripts and Nextflow processes.

. . .

But what do you do when you don't know the **expected output**?

---

## Domain invariants — first tool

Properties that must hold true after every run, regardless of the input.

```python
assert (df["value"] >= 0).all()           # positive values
assert total_out <= total_in              # filter doesn't create rows
assert len(ids) == len(set(ids))          # no duplicates
```

::: {.notes}
Methodological reference: Kamali et al. (2015) "How to test bioinformatics software?" — Biophysical Reviews 7(3):343–352. PMID:28510230. DOI:10.1007/s12551-015-0177-3
:::


---

## But invariants are not always enough

A tool can satisfy all invariants... and still produce biologically incorrect results.

. . .

> BWA-MEM and Bowtie2 produced **different** alignments depending on the order of reads in the input.
> The SAM files were valid. All reads were present. No visible error.
>
> — [Giannoulatou et al. 2014, BMC Bioinformatics](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4290646/)

. . .

On real data, it is not always possible to verify whether each individual result is correct.

→ This is the **oracle problem**.

---

## Three strategies for the oracle problem {.smaller}

| Approach | Principle |
|----------|----------|
| **Golden dataset** | Synthetic data — output defined *by construction* |
| **Pseudo-oracle** | Compare two independent implementations |
| **Metamorphic testing** | Verify a relation between two runs |

---

## Golden dataset

A synthetic dataset with the expected result defined by construction.

```
data/
├── sample.fastq          # 100 synthetic reads
├── sample_expected.txt   # frozen expected result
```

```bash
@test "golden dataset — count matches reference result" {
    run bash count_reads.sh sample.fastq
    [ "$output" = "$(cat sample_expected.txt)" ]
}
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Small, stable, versioned in the repo. Useful for long-term regression testing.
:::

---

## Pseudo-oracle

Compare two independent implementations on the same input.

```bash
@test "pseudo-oracle — two tools give the same read count" {
    count_A=$(tool_A sample.fastq | awk 'NR%4==1' | wc -l)
    count_B=$(tool_B sample.fastq | awk 'NR%4==1' | wc -l)
    [ "$count_A" -eq "$count_B" ]
}
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
The anvi'o / MicrobeAnnotator case (module 01) is a pseudo-oracle: two tools annotate the same genome — unexpected discrepancies revealed bugs.
:::

---

## Metamorphic testing: the idea

Verify a **relation** between two runs rather than an absolute value.

```
f(input_A)  ──┐
              ├── expected relation ?
f(input_B)  ──┘
```

::: {.notes}
Founding reference applied to bioinformatics: Chen et al. (2009) "An innovative approach for testing bioinformatics programs using metamorphic testing" — BMC Bioinformatics 10:24. PMID:19152705. PMC:PMC2657898.
:::

---

## Metamorphic relations (MR) {.smaller}

| MR | Input A → Input B | Relation |
|----|-------------------|----------|
| **Permutation** | reads → reordered reads | same count in output |
| **Duplication** | 100 reads → 200 reads | exactly 2× in output |
| **Subset** | 100 reads → 50 reads | output ≤ 100 |

---

## Example — bats

```bash
count_reads() { awk 'NR%4==1' "$1" | wc -l; }

@test "MR1 — permutation: order does not affect count" {
    [ "$(count_reads sample.fastq)" -eq "$(count_reads sample_shuffled.fastq)" ]
}
@test "MR2 — duplication: 2× input → 2× output" {
    original=$(count_reads sample.fastq)
    [ "$(count_reads sample_doubled.fastq)" -eq $(( original * 2 )) ]
}
@test "MR3 — subset: count ≤ original count" {
    [ "$(count_reads sample_subset.fastq)" -le "$(count_reads sample.fastq)" ]
}
```

---

## Key takeaway

::: {.callout-important}
**Test properties, not just values.**

Invariants are the foundation. Facing the oracle problem:
golden dataset + pseudo-oracle + metamorphic = three complementary strategies.
:::

---

## Next → Module 07

You now know how to write tests at every level — scripts, processes, workflows, and without an exact oracle.

. . .

The final question: **where to start and what to prioritise** in your team?

→ Module 07 — Strategy for a small team.

---

## Exercise → Module 06

Go to `exercises/06_metamorphic/`

The exercise focuses on **metamorphic testing** — the newest of the three patterns.

- 1 MR1 test — permutation → same count
- 1 MR2 test — duplication → 2× count
- 1 MR3 test — subset → count ≤ original

Bonus: apply the golden dataset pattern to the same data.
